Military Apr 4, 2026

Why The C-5M Super Galaxy's 28 Wheels Must Rotate 90 Degrees Just To Retract

The brilliant engineering solution enables a massive payload and efficient flight performance.

The C-5M's wheel rotation mechanism exemplifies how extreme cargo capacity demands drive unconventional landing gear designs that fundamentally reshape aircraft architecture.

See Simple Flying →

Military Apr 4, 2026

Iran Attained A Fleet Of F-14s Even Though The US Regretted It – Now They’re Destroyed

During the Cold War, Iran was viewed as a crucial stop-block for Soviet southern expansion, the F-14 played a role in this effort.

Iran's operational F-14 fleet, a legacy of pre-1979 US military support, represented a significant regional air power capability that Western aviation planners now recognize as a strategic miscalculation.

See Simple Flying →

Light Dynamix – Development Drone/UAV Engineer

We are looking for a practical, capable Development Engineer to join our team in a hands-on engineering...

Specialized UAV engineering roles are becoming permanent positions within the industry, signaling growth in commercial drone applications beyond experimental phases.

Safety Apr 4, 2026

Selecting Risk Controls and Accepting Residual Risk

Where Safety Risk Management moves from analysis to action — how organizations develop controls under §5.55(c)–(d).

Organizations must document their risk acceptance decisions and control selection rationale to demonstrate compliance with regulatory safety management requirements beyond initial hazard identification.

Commercial Apr 4, 2026

The 7 Longest Boeing 787 Dreamliner Routes In The World

Discover the seven longest Boeing 787 flights of 2026 and the airlines that make them possible.

Ultra-long-range 787 operations are becoming a competitive advantage for airlines seeking to capture premium international markets without traditional hub dependencies.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 4, 2026

Why Hawaiian Airlines Pilots Must Shave Their Beards During Alaska Integration

Hawaiian pilots will no longer be allowed beards under new Alaska uniform standards.

Alaska's stricter grooming standards for Hawaiian pilots highlight how airline mergers create operational friction beyond scheduling and fleet integration.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 4, 2026

Artemis 2 in good shape cruising towards the moon

A day after lighting its engine to head to the moon, the Artemis 2 Orion spacecraft is performing well with only minor issues.

Artemis 2's successful trans-lunar injection with minimal anomalies validates NASA's deep-space propulsion systems and crew safety protocols for sustained lunar operations.

See SpaceNews →

PAI
Safety Apr 4, 2026

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern SMS shifts aviation from post-accident investigation to predictive risk identification, fundamentally changing how operators prevent incidents before they occur.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Regulatory Apr 4, 2026

Trump budget seeks $372 million cut to US Essential Air Service program

US President Donald Trump’s fiscal 2027 budget proposal takes aim at the Essential Air Service program, calling for…...

Reducing EAS funding could force rural airports to lose commercial service entirely, threatening regional economic connectivity and emergency response capabilities.

See AeroTime Hub →

Military Apr 4, 2026

What It’s Like Flying In A Military Transport Aircraft

Uncle Sam's no-frills flying experience.

Military transport prioritizes payload and operational efficiency over passenger comfort, reflecting fundamentally different design priorities than civilian aviation.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 3, 2026

What United Airlines' New Relax Row Economy Beds Will Likely Cost On Long-Haul Flights

These beds could come with an appealing price tag.

United's economy bed pricing strategy could capture price-sensitive long-haul passengers willing to pay premium fares without upgrading to business class, reshaping revenue management models across carriers.

See Simple Flying →

▶
Safety Apr 4, 2026

Flying in the Unforgiving Wire Environment (The Rotorcraft Collective)

Many pilots mistakenly believe they will have sufficient reaction time if they encounter power lines and other wires. Yet wire strikes have been among the top three causes of fatal helicopter...

Wire strikes consistently rank among helicopter fatalities despite pilot overconfidence in avoidance capability, indicating a critical gap between perceived and actual reaction time.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

NASA Sets Coverage for Northrop Grumman’s CRS-24 Resupply Launch

NASA, Northrop Grumman, and SpaceX are targeting no earlier than 8:49 a.m. EDT on Wednesday, April 8, for the next launch delivering science investigations, supplies, and equipment to the International Space Station. Filled with approximately 11,000 pounds of cargo, the Northrop Grumman Cygnus XL...

Northrop Grumman's larger Cygnus XL variant will deliver 11,000 pounds of cargo, representing increased payload capacity for commercial ISS resupply missions.

See NASA →

Commercial Apr 3, 2026

6 Airlines With The World’s Longest Economy Seat Pitch

Which airlines are currently offering the most seat pitch for their respective economy class products? Here are 6 airlines that have mastered space.

Airlines competing on seat pitch are differentiating economy products as capacity pressures mount, signaling a strategic shift from pure revenue maximization toward passenger experience as competitive advantage.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

Why Do Airlines Still Use 3-3 Seating On Narrowbody Jets?

Narrowbody aircraft are limited by regulations to max out at 3-3 seating, while changing their fundamental design means they are not narrowbodies.

Regulatory certification rules lock airlines into 3-3 layouts because exceeding this density reclassifies aircraft as widebodies, forcing costly recertification and redesign.

See Simple Flying →

▶
Safety Apr 4, 2026

RNO Mustang 9 Departure

Pilots departing from runways 17 left and right at Reno-Tahoe International need to pay special attention to the Mustang 9 Departure. It is extremely important that the procedure on the Mustang 9...

The Mustang 9 Departure from RNO's parallel runways 17L/R requires heightened pilot attention due to specific procedural complexities that could impact safety if not properly executed.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Military Apr 3, 2026

US A-10 hit during Iran rescue mission, pilot ejects and is rescued

A US Air Force A-10 was hit during combat operations tied to the widening war with Iran on…...

Combat damage to legacy A-10s during sustained Iran operations highlights vulnerabilities of aging airframes in modern air defense environments.

See AeroTime Hub →

Military Apr 3, 2026

The World’s Largest Air Forces By Annual Budget

A closer look at the world leaders in air power spending.

Global air force spending concentration reveals strategic priorities: the top five nations control over 60% of worldwide military aviation budgets, directly shaping geopolitical competition and procurement patterns.

See Simple Flying →

Military Apr 3, 2026

US Air Force A-10 Thunderbolt II Crashes Amid Search For Downed F-15 Eagle

The pilot was successfully rescued.

The loss of an A-10 during combat search-and-rescue operations underscores the persistent vulnerability of legacy platforms in modern threat environments.

See Simple Flying →

Industry Apr 4, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across integrated airline-airport systems offers faster environmental gains than waiting for new aircraft technology adoption across fleets.

See MDPI →

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

Michael Graham Named NTSB Vice Chairman

The certified transport pilot and NTSB board member will co-head the agency for a three-year term.

Graham's operational pilot background will strengthen the NTSB's technical credibility in investigating complex aviation accidents during a critical period for aerospace safety.

See AVweb →

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

US and Europe revise GPS interference guidelines as spoofing risks grow

Aviation authorities are warning that instances of GPS interference have spread across a widening range of high-risk airspace,…...

Growing GPS spoofing incidents across European and US airspace are prompting regulators to strengthen interference detection standards, making robust backup navigation systems critical for operators.

See AeroTime Hub →

Military Apr 3, 2026

A-10 Thunderbolt II Crashed Near the Strait of Hormuz, U.S. Officials Tell NYT

Almost the same time as an F-15E Strike Eagle went down over Iran, the U.S. Air Force lost an A-10 Thunderbolt II attack aircraft near the Strait of Hormuz. U.S. officials told NYT that the jet’s pilot was safely rescued. While search and rescue efforts for at least one of the crew members of an […]...

Two U.S. Air Force combat aircraft losses in the same region highlight operational intensity and risks during Middle East contingencies despite successful pilot recovery.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Meet NASA’s New Artemis II Science Officers

NASA’s first Artemis II science officers—Kelsey Young, Trevor Graff, and Angela Garcia—are pioneering a new era of mission operations by integrating advanced lunar science directly into Mission Control. Their work will shape how future Artemis crews observe, analyze, and deepen our understanding of...

Embedding dedicated science officers in Mission Control represents a fundamental shift in lunar operations, enabling real-time scientific decision-making that could accelerate discovery cycles for future deep-space exploration programs.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Italy’s Argotec plans to scale Florida satellite facility to meet rising US demand

Italy’s Argotec has officially opened its first U.S. satellite production facility, cementing a foothold near Kennedy Space Center in Florida to join other foreign space firms pursuing growing demand from American programs.

European satellite manufacturers are establishing U.S. production bases to directly serve American defense and commercial space programs, reducing supply chain dependencies and capturing domestic market opportunities.

See SpaceNews →

Military Apr 3, 2026

US F-15E Shot Down Over Iran, One Crew Member Rescued

The fate of the aircraft’s second crew member is unknown.

Loss of a crewed fighter in contested airspace raises critical questions about modern air defense capabilities and pilot survivability protocols in high-threat environments.

See AVweb →

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

FAA report proposes sweeping rewrite of Part 141 flight training rules

A 471-page industry report released through the FAA’s Part 141 modernization docket proposes a sweeping rewrite of how…...

Comprehensive regulatory overhaul aims to streamline outdated flight training standards that haven't fundamentally changed in decades, potentially accelerating pilot certification timelines.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Virgil I. “Gus” Grissom

Today marks the 100th anniversary of the birth of Virgil I. “Gus” Grissom, born April 3, 1926, in Mitchell, Indiana. As one of NASA’s first seven astronauts, he became America’s second astronaut to fly in space when he launched aboard the Liberty Bell 7 spacecraft on July 21, 1961, just weeks after...

Gus Grissom's pioneering suborbital flight in 1961 established critical human spaceflight protocols that remain foundational to modern aerospace operations and crew safety standards.

See NASA →

Military Apr 3, 2026

Picture of the Day: April 3, 2026

A C-130J awaits its next assignment on the flightline.

The C-130J remains operationally active and mission-ready, underscoring the aircraft's continued relevance in military transport operations decades after its introduction.

See AVweb →

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

ZeroAvia edges closer to cert as FAA issues electric engine conditions

ZeroAvia has this week celebrated a “major step” towards certification of its ZA600 hydrogen-electric powertrain, after the U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) published special conditions to certify the system’s electric engine component. The FAA published 33 special conditions for...

FAA's 33 special conditions establish the regulatory framework for hydrogen-electric propulsion certification, accelerating ZeroAvia's path to commercial deployment in regional aviation.

See Runway Girl Network →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

White House again proposes steep NASA budget cuts

For the second consecutive year, the White House is proposing a major budget cut for NASA that would significantly impact the agency’s science programs and the International Space Station.

Sustained NASA budget cuts threaten ISS operations and space industry supply chains that aerospace contractors depend on for sustained growth.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Test Pilots and the Road Back to the Moon

For NASA’s first crewed lunar mission since Apollo, aviation experience is one of the clearest threads linking the astronauts aboard Orion.

NASA's Artemis astronaut selection prioritizes operational test-flying experience, reflecting lessons learned that deep aviation expertise improves mission success and problem-solving in unprecedented exploration scenarios.

See AVweb →

Drones Apr 3, 2026

New DJI Terra update adds HEIF support, boosts reconstruction performance

DJI is continuing to refine its mapping and modeling ecosystem with the latest update to DJI Terra, and version 5.2.0 brings a mix of speed boosts, smarter automation, and improved output quality for enterprise users. more…...

DJI Terra's performance improvements and HEIF support optimization enable faster, higher-quality 3D reconstructions for commercial drone operators, reducing processing time for industrial inspection and survey missions.

See DroneDJ →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Hello, World

NASA astronaut and Artemis II Commander Reid Wiseman took this picture of Earth from the Orion spacecraft’s window after completing the translunar injection burn. There are two auroras (top right and bottom left) and zodiacal light (bottom right) is visible as the Earth eclipses the Sun. This and...

Orion's advanced window technology enables unprecedented Earth observation capabilities that could inform climate monitoring and emergency response systems during deep-space missions.

See NASA →

Military Apr 3, 2026

US rescues one airman after F-15E shot down over Iran, search continues for second

Unverified images circulating on social media on April 3, 2026, appear to show the wreckage of a US…...

Loss of advanced fighter aircraft over hostile territory creates urgent operational demands for search-and-rescue coordination and potential diplomatic complications affecting military aviation readiness.

See AeroTime Hub →

Drones Apr 3, 2026

US drone future hinges on policy, investment, coordination

The Commercial Drone Alliance is pushing Washington to move faster, and more strategically, on building a truly domestic drone ecosystem. In a newly released white paper, the industry group calls on the Trump Administration to adopt sweeping reforms aimed at strengthening US manufacturing, creating...

US drone competitiveness requires coordinated federal action on manufacturing and infrastructure, not just regulatory approval, to prevent Chinese dominance in this emerging sector.
